name: Italy
summary: Home to continental Europe's largest Bangladeshi community (~150,000–200,000, centred on Rome) with abundant halal and a dense BD ecosystem, world-class affordable study (Politecnico di Milano at ~€1–4k tuition with generous scholarships), SSN healthcare, a full embassy in Dhaka, dual citizenship retained both ways — and, the strongest lever for this profile, a quota-exempt EU Blue Card direct-work route that sidesteps the oversubscribed Decreto Flussi click-day. Held back by a 10-year naturalisation path (a 2025 referendum to cut it to 5 failed), low Italian tech salaries, heavy Sportello Unico/Questura bureaucracy, and the Meloni/League government's anti-Islam posture.

links.title: Universitaly — official portal for studying in Italy
Polizia di Stato — residence permits (Permesso di soggiorno, official)

job-market.summary: AI/ML demand is surging in Milan/Rome/Turin (a reported 625% rise, AI engineers ~€78k) with €10k–20k premiums on hot skills, so a well-placed AI engineer hub-side is well absorbed; but general Italian tech salaries stay low by Northern-European standards, hiring is bureaucratic, and Italian is usually required.

pros.text: AI/ML demand is surging (a reported 625% rise in ML job demand); AI engineers average ~€78k (Milan +11%), and hubs in Milan, Rome and Turin are integrating AI into finance/healthcare.
Hot AI/cloud/data skills command €10k–20k premiums.

cons.text: General software-engineer pay is low (~€46k average; entry ~€23–45k) versus Northern Europe.
Italian is usually required outside multinationals, and hiring/bureaucracy is slow.

links.title: Nucamp — best-paid tech jobs in Italy (2025)
Levels.fyi — Software Engineer salary, Milan

visa-access.summary: A clear strength: Italy has a full embassy in Dhaka and a well-trodden Bangladeshi pipeline (Universitaly pre-enrolment), with modest funds — though Questura/embassy processing is slow.

pros.text: Italy has a full embassy in Dhaka and an exceptionally well-established Bangladeshi migration pipeline; students pre-enrol via Universitaly with modest proof of funds.
Bangladesh features in Italy's decreto-flussi labour quotas, reflecting strong bilateral ties.

cons.text: Italian visa and Questura processing is notoriously slow and bureaucratic, with significant 2026 backlogs.

links.title: Universitaly — pre-enrolment for international students (official)
Ministry of Labour — the Bangladeshi community in Italy (annual report)

citizenship.summary: Dual citizenship is retained both ways and the passport is elite (Henley 4th), but naturalisation requires 10 years' residence plus B1 Italian — and a June-2025 referendum to cut it to 5 failed.

pros.text: Italy has permitted dual citizenship without restriction since 1992 and is on Bangladesh's DNC list, so a naturalising Bangladeshi keeps BD nationality.
The Italian passport ranks joint 4th on Henley 2026 (~185 destinations).

cons.text: Naturalisation requires 10 years' legal residence (among the longest in the EU) plus a B1 Italian exam and tax/income history — a June-2025 referendum to cut it to 5 years failed on turnout.
Processing of residence-based citizenship applications is slow.

links.title: Bocca di Turi — Italian citizenship by residency (2025 updates)

post-study-work.summary: Graduates get a 12-month job-search residence permit (permesso per attesa occupazione — any degree level, no offer needed) that allows work or self-employment and converts cleanly to a work permit (incl. the quota-exempt Blue Card), with each spouse qualifying independently; the 12-month cap and Questura backlogs are the real limits.

pros.text: A 12-month job-search residence permit (permesso per attesa occupazione) is available to any Italian-degree graduate with no job offer required, allowing work or self-employment; each spouse qualifies.

cons.text: The window is only 12 months, and Questura administrative backlogs can eat into it.

links.title: Italy Handbook — post-graduation work permit options

spouse-family.summary: Italy has generous family-reunification (ricongiungimento) with spouse work rights and free schooling, reinforced by deep Bangladeshi family networks.

pros.text: Family reunification (ricongiungimento familiare) is well established, the joining spouse can work, and children attend public school free; if both spouses study, each holds their own permit.
Deep Bangladeshi family/community networks ease settlement.

cons.text: Reunification carries income/housing-adequacy conditions and bureaucratic delays.

links.title: Polizia di Stato — family reunification (official)

msc-study.summary: A clear standout: Politecnico di Milano (top-~40 CS, ~45 English-taught MSc) and Bologna offer English-taught CS/AI at very low income-based tuition (~€1–4k) with generous scholarships (up to €25k); a BD BSc-CSE is admissible via Universitaly and students may work ~20 hrs/week.

pros.text: Politecnico di Milano (top-~40 CS globally) runs ~45 English-taught MSc programmes; non-EU public tuition is income-based at only ~€1,000–4,000/yr, with generous PoliMi scholarships (up to €25k packages).
University of Bologna (top-200 CS) adds depth; a Bangladeshi BSc-CSE is admissible via Universitaly and students may work ~20 hrs/week.

cons.text: Italian-language daily life and bureaucracy add friction even for English-taught students.

links.title: Politecnico di Milano — scholarships (official)
Politecnico di Torino — applicants with a non-Italian qualification (official)

pr-pathway.summary: EU long-term residence is reachable after 5 years, but it requires Italian-language proof and runs through slow, bureaucratic Questura processing.

pros.text: The EU long-term residence permit (permesso di soggiorno UE per soggiornanti di lungo periodo) is reachable after 5 years with adequate income.

cons.text: It requires an A2/B1 Italian-language test and is processed through slow, backlog-prone Questure.

links.title: Polizia di Stato — long-term residence permit (official)

income-cost.summary: Low salaries are partly offset by lower living costs (outside Milan) and very low tuition, leaving a modest surplus.

pros.text: Very low tuition and lower living costs outside Milan make the study phase affordable, and a large BD community offers cheap settlement support.

cons.text: Low Italian tech salaries cap the dual-earner surplus and savings/remittance potential; Milan housing is pricey.

links.title: Mastersportal — tuition & living costs in Italy

healthcare.summary: The SSN gives registered residents/students low-cost universal care, with regional quality variation and longer waits in the south.

pros.text: The Servizio Sanitario Nazionale (SSN) gives registered residents and students low-cost universal care (voluntary student enrolment is inexpensive).

cons.text: Quality varies sharply by region, with longer waits in the south, and family/childcare benefits are modest.

links.title: Italy — Servizio Sanitario Nazionale (overview)

culture-language.summary: Italian is essential for daily life and status, and English is limited — but an enormous established Bangladeshi community makes adaptation easier than the language barrier suggests.

pros.text: Excellent lifestyle, climate and food, and an enormous established Bangladeshi community (Rome and beyond) that dramatically eases cultural adaptation.

cons.text: Italian is essential for daily life, work and status (B1 for citizenship); English is of limited everyday use.
The Meloni/League political climate cools acceptance, even if local diaspora life is strong.

links.title: Bangladeshis in Italy (overview)

safety-law.summary: Mid-tier rule of law (WJP ~32nd) with regional crime and slow justice, and weak enforcement of anti-discrimination protections.

pros.text: Generally safe day-to-day in most cities, with a strong passport and EU legal framework.

cons.text: Mid-tier rule of law (WJP ~32nd) with corruption, very slow civil justice and regional organised-crime issues.
Anti-discrimination protections are weakly enforced, and anti-migrant incidents occur.

links.title: World Justice Project — Rule of Law Index 2025

politics.summary: A stable but hard-right government (Meloni's Fratelli d'Italia with the League) that is restrictive and anti-Islam in posture, even as Italy relies on migrant labour.

pros.text: Unusually for Italy the government is stable, and labour-migration quotas (decreto flussi) have been expanded because the economy needs migrant workers.

cons.text: Giorgia Meloni's Fratelli d'Italia governs with the anti-immigration League, with a restrictive and anti-Islam posture.
The League has long obstructed mosque openings, and Islam lacks official religious recognition.

links.title: Free Press — Italy is Muslim-friendly, but its PM is not

tax.summary: A heavy wedge (IRPEF to 43% plus regional and social contributions), softened by the impatriate regime (now 50% exemption after a 2024 cut) and an Italy–Bangladesh treaty.

pros.text: The impatriate regime (regime impatriati) still offers a 50% income exemption for qualifying inbound workers, and an Italy–Bangladesh double-taxation treaty supports repatriation.

cons.text: The overall tax/social wedge is heavy (IRPEF to 43% plus regional surtaxes and INPS), and the impatriate relief was cut from 70% to 50% and tightened in 2024.

links.title: Italy — Agenzia delle Entrate (tax authority)

community-belonging.summary: Continental Europe's largest Bangladeshi community (~150,000–200,000, centred on Rome) with a dense halal/BD-business ecosystem — but Islam lacks official recognition, so formal mosques are scarce and the climate is politically hostile.

pros.text: Italy hosts ~150,000–200,000 Bangladeshis — continental Europe's largest BD community (Rome's is the biggest), with a dense ecosystem of BD groceries, eateries and halal food across Lazio, Lombardy and Veneto.

cons.text: Islam is not among the 13 officially recognised religions, so there are fewer than 10 officially recognised mosques and prayer often happens in informal spaces — and the League/Meloni climate obstructs new mosques.

links.title: Bangladeshis in Italy (overview)
France 24 — no place to pray for Muslim workers in an Italian city

direct-work-route.summary: The decisive lever for a BD software engineer is the EU Blue Card (Carta Blu UE): it sits entirely OUTSIDE the annual Decreto Flussi quota, so an employer can sponsor a Dhaka-based engineer year-round rather than fighting an oversubscribed click-day. A recognised CS/IT degree (or 3 years' relevant IT experience in the prior 7) plus a 6-month+ contract above the salary floor (~1.5x the ISTAT average, ~€35,000–38,300 in 2025–26; a reduced ~1.2x ~€28,000 applies to ICT/shortage roles) — easily cleared by Milan software pay (mid ~€42–62k, senior ~€83k). The employer files the nulla osta at the Sportello Unico via the Ministry of Interior ALI portal; the worker collects the visa at the consulate in Bangladesh, then signs the contratto di soggiorno within 8 days of arrival. Route is a clear settlement track: Blue Card -> EU long-term residence after 5 years (min 2 in Italy) -> citizenship at 10 years + B1 Italian, with dual citizenship retained both ways (Italy on Bangladesh's DNC list). Headwinds: heavy Sportello Unico/Questura bureaucracy, a labour-market test that is often (not always) waived, low Blue Card uptake (only ~747 issued in 2023) so the direct-skilled-work track record is thin even though the overall BD community (~150,000+, continental Europe's largest) is enormous, and a slow 10-year citizenship clock.

pros.text: EU Blue Card is QUOTA-EXEMPT — outside the oversubscribed Decreto Flussi click-day — so an Italian employer can sponsor and relocate a BD software engineer directly from Dhaka at any time of year; a recognised CS degree (or 3 yrs IT experience) plus a 6-month+ contract above ~€35,000–38,300 (reduced ~€28,000 for ICT/shortage roles) qualifies, and Milan software salaries clear it.

Complete, dual-retained settlement chain: Blue Card -> EU long-term residence after 5 years (min 2 in Italy) -> citizenship at 10 years + B1, with one of continental Europe's largest Bangladeshi communities (~150,000+, Bangladeshis now top-3 for new residence permits) as a deep landing network.

cons.text: Blue Card uptake is low (~747 issued in 2023), so the direct-skilled-work track record is thin (the huge BD community arrived mainly via Decreto Flussi/family/asylum, not highly-qualified routes); employers face Sportello Unico/Questura bureaucracy and a labour-market test that is only often, not always, waived — dampening sponsorship appetite outside Milan multinationals.
Citizenship is a slow 10-year clock (B1 Italian; a June-2025 referendum to cut it to 5 failed), and standard salaried hiring outside the Blue Card stays trapped in the quota lottery.
